topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink on the paper felt like a physical stain on my chest. A C-minus. I’d poured weeks into that history paper, meticulously citing sources, wrestling with complex arguments. This result felt fundamentally wrong. I reread the feedback, my eyes darting over the critiques. They were vague, almost dismissive—a suggestion that my thesis lacked 'depth' and that my analysis was 'superficial.' I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight. I picked up my pen, tracing the margin of the grade with a fingertip. Every word seemed to hold a hidden meaning, a deliberate misdirection. I started re-reading my own work, searching for the flaw, the hidden error I had overlooked. My jaw tightened as I scrutinized the rubric again, wondering if I had fundamentally misunderstood the assignment parameters, or if something else entirely had gone amiss in the grading process.

[story 2]

When I saw the mark, my breath hitched. It was a disaster. I stared at the numerical score, the letters blurring into an indistinct smear of disappointment. My hands started to tremble, a slight vibration I tried to suppress by gripping the edge of my desk until my knuckles were white. I didn't look at the professor immediately; instead, I began methodically reviewing the submission, line by line, as if the text itself held the answer to the discrepancy. There were no glaring errors, yet the evaluation seemed to have missed the core of my effort. A cold knot formed in my stomach. I started pacing the small space between my desk and the window, my footsteps echoing too loudly in the quiet room. "That can't be right," I muttered to the empty air, the sound thin and shaky. I felt an urgent need to confirm that my understanding of the material was accurate, that the assessment reflected reality, not some arbitrary judgment.

[story 3]

The notification flashed across my screen, and the color drained from my face. A significantly lower mark than I had anticipated. I blinked hard, trying to clear the fog that had suddenly descended. My fingers hovered over the mouse, reluctant to click on the detailed breakdown. Instead, I let my gaze drift around the classroom. Everyone else seemed to be packing up, exchanging casual farewells. I focused intently on the professor as they gathered their notes, noting the subtle shift in their posture—a slight stiffness, a quick glance away when I approached. A prickle of unease ran down my spine. I forced a calm expression onto my features, offering a polite, small nod. "Professor," I began, my voice carefully level, "could I perhaps clarify a point regarding the assessment?" The question felt heavy, loaded with unspoken inquiry, as I waited for their response.
